label: Morning — Fall Foliage with Uplights
prompt:
Goal: Generate a photorealistic architectural rendering of the provided image, converting it into a morning setting with soft sunlight and natural fall foliage colors.

Geometry & Context:
• Maintain the original building geometry, façade proportions, and surrounding context exactly as shown.

Time of Day & Sky:
• Change the time of day to early morning with gentle, warm sunlight and long, soft shadows.
• Sky should be clear or slightly hazy with a subtle golden hue typical of morning light.

Foliage & Landscape:
• Apply realistic fall colors to trees and landscaping: muted tones of orange, amber, and golden yellow with some remaining green for balance.
• Avoid overly saturated or vibrant hues—keep colors natural and blended.
• Include a few scattered fallen leaves on paving for subtle realism.

Materials:
• Transparent glass with natural reflections
• Accurate brickwork with visible mortar depth
• Metal and concrete with realistic texture and light patina
• Paving with tonal variation

Lighting & Effects:
• Show very subtle uplights on the main building façade AND the back building façade for architectural emphasis (soft, not overexposed).
• Show cars in motion with slight motion blur.

Quality: Ultra-high-resolution, sharp focus, competition-board photoreal quality.
Output: PNG or TIFF.

Avoid: Overexposed lighting, unrealistic glow effects, altering building geometry.

label: Morning — Green Foliage (No Fall Colors) with Uplights
prompt:
Goal: Generate a photorealistic architectural rendering of the provided image, converting it into an early morning setting with soft sunlight and vibrant greenery.

Geometry & Context:
• Maintain the original building geometry, façade proportions, and surrounding context exactly as shown.
• Keep the same camera angle, focal length, and perspective from the original image.

Time of Day & Sky:
• Change the time of day to early morning (approx. 7:30 AM) with gentle, warm sunlight and long, soft shadows.
• Sky should be clear or slightly hazy with a subtle golden hue.
• Apply realistic color temperature (3500–4000K).

Foliage & Landscape:
• Use lush green landscaping with natural variation in tones — no fall colors or scattered leaves.

Materials:
• Transparent glass with natural reflections
• Accurate brickwork
• Metal and concrete surfaces
• Paving with tonal variation

Lighting & Effects:
• Show very subtle uplights on the main building façade AND the back building façade (soft, not overexposed).
• Show cars in motion with slight motion blur.

Quality: Ultra-high-resolution, physically based rendering (PBR).
Output: PNG or TIFF.

Avoid: Overexposed lighting, unrealistic glow effects, altering building geometry.

label: Dusk — Dynamic Motion Elements
prompt:

Geometry & Context:

Time of Day & Sky:

Materials:
• Transparent glass with natural reflections

Lighting & Effects:

• Show cars in motion with slight motion blur.

Quality: Ultra-high-resolution, sharp focus, competition-board photoreal quality.
Output: PNG or TIFF.

Avoid: Overexposed lighting, unrealistic glow effects, altering building geometry, washing out dusk sky gradients.

• Add street lights and decorative string/fairy lights for ambiance.

Materials:

label: Daytime — Crisp Natural Lighting
prompt:
Goal: Generate a photorealistic architectural rendering of the provided image, converting it into a bright daytime setting with crisp, natural lighting and vibrant outdoor details.

Geometry & Context:
• Maintain the original building geometry, façade proportions, and surrounding context exactly as shown.

Time of Day & Sky:
• Change the time of day to mid-morning or early afternoon with clear, bright daylight.
• Sky should be clear or slightly cloudy with a natural blue tone.
• Apply realistic daylight color temperature (5000–6500K).

Materials:
• Transparent glass with natural reflections
• Accurate brickwork with visible mortar depth

label: Daytime — Cinematic Sun Direction & Shadows
prompt:
Convert this image to a dynamic daytime scene with strong, realistic sunlight.

• Use a clear sun direction (e.g., sun from upper-left) and create accurate, crisp shadows cast by the building’s edges, balconies, and window reveals.
• Add subtle ambient bounce light in shadowed areas.
• Preserve surface materials.
• Maintain realistic contrast and color temperature.

label: Colored Pencil + Watercolour — Option 1 (Quick)
prompt:
Generate a stylized, artistic rendering and water color effect of the provided image in a colored pencil style.

Building Treatment:
• The main building must retain its exact original colors, textures, and architectural details, preserving a historic preservation aesthetic.

Surrounding Context:
• Surrounding elements such as streetscape, trees, and context can be rendered in soft, expressive colored pencil strokes, emphasizing artistic texture and hand-drawn quality.

General:
• Maintain accurate scale and proportions of all elements, while creating a visually appealing, painterly illustration.

Output: High resolution with clear line definition and colored pencil shading style.

label: Colored Pencil + Watercolour — Option 2 (Detailed)
prompt:
Goal: Generate a sketchy yet detailed architectural visualization of the provided image, combining watercolor softness with colored pencil texture for an artistic rendering with controlled ground-floor treatment.

Building Treatment:
• Maintain the building's exact original colors, textures, proportions, and architectural details.
• If the building has a visible ground-floor retail, lobby, or amenity area, render it with slight transparency, warm interior tones, and sketchy line accents to suggest activity behind the glass.

Graphic Style & Technique:
Create a stylized, artistic rendering with a blend of:
• Colored pencil effect
• Soft watercolor washes
• Sketchy architectural linework

Surrounding Context:
• Use expressive, hand-drawn strokes primarily on streetscape elements, trees, greenery, and general context.
• The main building should remain more detailed and true to its real textures, but with light sketch accents.
• Keep the scale and proportions of all elements accurate.
• The building should retain its exact original color palette and material definition, even within the stylized effect.
• Apply more looseness and artistic texture to the surroundings while keeping the building crisp and legible.

Output:
• High-resolution rendering
• Clear line definition
• Visible colored pencil shading and watercolor layering

Avoid: Overly realistic photorealism, changing building geometry/color/massing, overblurring or cartoon-like effects.

label: Sketchy, Artistic Site Plan Rendering (Watercolor + Colored Pencil)
prompt:
Generate a sketchy yet slightly detailed architectural SITE PLAN visualization of the provided image. The output should read as a top‑down plan view, combining watercolor softness with colored pencil texture for an artistic, hand‑drawn architectural representation.
This rendering should feel suitable for concept presentations, site analysis boards, or early design studies, not a photoreal render.

View & Geometry:
• Maintain a strict 2D / top‑down site plan view (no perspective, no eye‑level elements).
• Keep all site geometry, building footprints, circulation paths, and open spaces exactly the same.
• Do not alter scale, proportions, or orientation.

Graphic Style & Technique:
Create a stylized architectural drawing using a blend of:
• Colored pencil shading for surfaces and edges
• Soft watercolor washes for ground planes and landscape
• Sketchy architectural linework with slight variation in line weight
The style should feel hand‑drawn, expressive, and intentionally imperfect, while remaining clear and legible.

Building Representation:
• Show building footprints and massing outlines only (no façades, no floor‑level details).
• Keep buildings more crisp and controlled than the surroundings.
• Maintain the exact original color palette and material logic, interpreted graphically (light fills, soft textures).
• Add subtle sketch lines or pencil hatching to indicate mass without heavy detail.

Site & Landscape (Primary Focus):
Apply more artistic looseness to the site context:
• Streets, sidewalks, plazas, and paving rendered with loose pencil strokes and watercolor fills.
• Trees and greenery illustrated with hand‑drawn organic shapes, soft green watercolor tones, and sketchy outlines.
• Open spaces emphasized through calm washes and texture rather than hard linework.
Avoid photoreal textures—everything should feel illustrative and diagrammatic.

Simplification Rules:
Remove unnecessary details such as:
• Furniture symbols
• Curb annotations
• Excessive CAD linework
• Technical notes
Retain only essential site information needed to understand layout and organization.

Color & Tone:
• Muted, architectural color palette.
• Light paper‑like background.
• Soft shadows or tonal depth only where needed to define edges and massing.
• No strong contrast or heavy shading.

Output Requirements:
• High‑resolution, presentation‑ready image.
• Clear line definition.
• Visible colored pencil texture and watercolor softness.
• Suitable for architectural boards and design narratives.

Avoid:
• Photorealism
• Perspective or 3D views
• Façade or elevation details
• Interior lighting or transparency
• Over‑detailing or clutter
• Dark, heavy shadows or digital effects

label: 3D Massing Model to Sketchy Watercolour Render
prompt:
Goal: Convert a 3D massing model screenshot into a sketchy architectural visualization that reads as a hand-drawn concept illustration, combining watercolor softness with colored pencil texture.

Input Handling:
• The provided image is a 3D massing model (Rhino, SketchUp, Revit, or similar) — it may have flat or minimal textures.
• Maintain the exact building massing, proportions, volume relationships, and camera angle from the model.
• Do not alter or simplify the building form — treat the 3D geometry as the single source of truth.

Building Treatment:
• Interpret the massing surfaces with implied materials: concrete, glass, metal, or masonry based on visual cues.
• Add subtle pencil hatching and light watercolor washes to define planes and depth.
• Differentiate wall planes from glass planes through tonal variation — glass should read as lighter, slightly reflective washes; solid walls as warmer, more textured fills.
• Emphasize edges and corners with heavier pencil linework to define the building's volume.

Graphic Style & Technique:
Create a stylized architectural drawing using a blend of:
• Colored pencil shading for surfaces and shadow areas
• Soft watercolor washes for sky, ground plane, and building faces
• Sketchy architectural linework with variation in weight — heavier on edges, lighter on surface detail
The style should feel like an architect's concept sketch, not a finished render.

Surrounding Context (if present):
• If the model includes site context, streets, or landscape, render them with expressive, loose strokes.
• Trees and greenery: hand-drawn organic shapes with soft green watercolor tones.
• Ground plane: light wash with subtle pencil texture, not detailed paving.
• If no context is present, add a simple ground shadow and light sky wash to ground the building.

Output:
• High-resolution, presentation-ready image
• Clear line definition with visible pencil texture
• Watercolor layering that reads as intentionally hand-crafted

Avoid: Photorealism, altering building massing or geometry, heavy dark shadows, cartoon-like effects, adding façade details not implied by the model.

label: Google Earth Aerial to Watercolour Site Plan
prompt:
Goal: Convert a Google Earth aerial screenshot into a stylized watercolor site plan suitable for design presentations, feasibility studies, or context analysis boards.

Input:
• The provided image is a top-down Google Earth screenshot. Maintain the exact same view, crop, scale, and orientation — do not reframe or zoom.

Graphic Style:
• Apply a soft watercolor wash effect across the entire image.
• Trees and vegetation: render in deeper, richer green tones with hand-drawn organic softness — darker than the original satellite imagery.
• Buildings: highlight with pencil sketch outlines to define footprints clearly against the landscape. Add light tonal fills to differentiate rooftops from ground.
• Roads and paving: render in lighter gray tones with subtle pencil texture — softer than the original.
• Water (if present): pale blue watercolor wash with minimal texture.
• Open ground and parking: light warm washes with subtle pencil grain.

Composition Rules:
• Do not crop or reframe the image — output must match the input boundary exactly.
• Do not fade or vignette the edges — maintain full detail to all four edges.
• Maintain all spatial relationships, street widths, and building positions exactly.

Output: High-resolution presentation-ready image with visible watercolor texture and pencil line definition.

Avoid: Cropping or reframing, edge fading or vignetting, photorealistic textures, overly dark or heavy rendering, altering the aerial viewpoint.

label: 3D Printed Architecture Model — Full Axon + Elevations
prompt:
Generate a high-fidelity 3D building model in the look of a ‘3D printed architecture model’ based on the provided architectural photo.

Colored Axonometric View:
• Preserve the building’s massing and key texture details, lightly stylized for a game.
• Render with realistic, physically based lighting and shadows.
• Show a 45-degree top-down (isometric) view to emphasize depth.
• Define materials clearly—reflective glass, metallic surfaces, concrete—so it reads as a high-quality, game-engine-ready render.
• Use a pure white background.

White Axonometric View:
• Remove all textures and make the model clean white, with lines to outline the details and modeling components.
• Render with realistic, physically based lighting and shadows.
• Show a 45-degree top-down (isometric) view to emphasize depth.
• Use a pure white background.

Front and Side Elevations:
• Include all buildings in the image.
• The elevations must be flat and 2D, not a perspective style.
• Keep the same clean white style with lines to outline details and modeling components.
• Render with realistic, physically based lighting and shadows.
• Use a pure white background.

label: Exploded Axonometric Technical Diagram
prompt:
Create a technical exploded axonometric diagram based on the building in the image.

• Visually decompose the building by separating its layers vertically, showing the structural grid, floor slabs, and facade elements as distinct floating parts.
• Render this as a black and white vector-style wireframe drawing with varying line weights and projected shadows to demonstrate the construction method.
• Convert the image into an isometric structural exploded diagram.

label: 2D Floor Plan to 3D Isometric Cutaway (Japandi Residential)
prompt:
OVERRIDE: The uploaded image is a FLAT 2D floor plan. Use it ONLY as a spatial data source for room layout, wall positions, door swings, window locations, furniture placement, and room proportions. COMPLETELY IGNORE the camera angle and orientation of the uploaded image. You MUST reproject all geometry into a new 3D viewpoint as defined below. Do NOT extrude the plan upward from its existing top-down view.

Goal: Generate a photorealistic isometric 3D cutaway render of the attached 2D floor plan, viewed as a dollhouse model from the SW corner. The output must precisely replicate the spatial layout of the input plan while applying the visual style defined below.

Geometry (from attached floor plan):
• Replicate every wall, door opening, door swing direction, window position, furniture piece, fixture, and appliance from the attached floor plan in exact position, proportion, count, and orientation.
• The floor plan is the absolute single source of truth for all geometry.
• Do not add, remove, rearrange, or resize any element.
• Maintain accurate room proportions — if one room is twice the width of another in the plan, it must be in the output.
• Preserve all corridor widths, partition thicknesses, door swing clearances, and opening positions exactly as drawn.
• If furniture is shown, match its exact grouping, count, and placement. If a zone is empty, keep it empty.

Camera — SW Corner (MANDATORY):
• Imagine the uploaded floor plan as a flat rectangle lying on a table. The camera is physically placed at the BOTTOM-LEFT CORNER of that rectangle, raised 55–60 degrees above the table surface, and aimed diagonally toward the TOP-RIGHT CORNER of the rectangle.
• This means the BOTTOM-LEFT CORNER of the plan is the nearest point to the viewer and sits at the BOTTOM of the output image. The TOP-RIGHT CORNER of the plan is the farthest point from the viewer and sits at the TOP of the output image.
• The LEFT EDGE of the plan runs diagonally from the bottom of the image upward toward the upper-left.
• The BOTTOM EDGE of the plan runs diagonally from the bottom of the image upward toward the upper-right.
• The plan reads as a diamond shape tilted away from the viewer, with its nearest corner pointing straight down toward the bottom of the frame.
• All four perimeter walls are visible and cut at low height. None are removed.
• True axonometric projection — all parallel lines remain parallel, zero perspective convergence.
• Frame the entire plan with generous white negative space on all sides.
• SELF-CHECK: The corner of the floor plan that is at the BOTTOM-LEFT of the uploaded 2D image MUST appear as the closest, lowest point in the output 3D render. If it does not, the camera is wrong — regenerate.

Section Cut (Dollhouse Style):
• ALL four perimeter walls AND all interior partition walls are cut at a uniform height of approximately 4–5 feet (just above door handle height).
• This creates a full dollhouse effect where every room is completely visible from above.
• No ceiling on any room — all rooms fully open to the sky.
• Wall section cut edges: clean, flat, and bright white on top.
• Wall thickness clearly visible at all cut edges (approximately 6–8 inches at scale).
• Door openings show doors modeled in their swing positions (open or ajar as indicated in the plan).

Exterior Slab & Wall Treatment:
• Floor slab visible at the perimeter with a clear edge thickness (approximately 8–10 inches at scale).
• Exterior face of the slab and the exterior side of all perimeter walls rendered in a cool light blue-gray tone — NOT white. This creates a subtle color distinction between the warm interior and the cooler structural shell.
• The slab edge and exterior wall color should read as a soft blue-gray concrete (#B0BEC5 to #90A4AE range).

Materials — Interior:
• Interior walls: smooth matte white plaster, clean and bright, no texture or marks.
• Flooring: light natural hardwood planks in warm maple or birch tone with visible grain, running bond pattern, consistent plank direction throughout the unit. Warm golden hue — not gray, not bleached, not orange.
• Kitchen: white upper and lower cabinetry with light wood base accents, white solid-surface or quartz countertop, stainless steel sink and appliances (gas range with visible burners, refrigerator), open shelving with kitchen items (dishes, jars, cutting boards, cookbooks, small plants).
• Bathroom: white tile flooring, white porcelain fixtures (toilet, vanity, tub or shower), glass shower enclosure if indicated, small accessories (mirror, toiletries, towels).
• Closets: clothes hanging on rods, folded items on shelves — visible and detailed, rendered as if doors are open.
• Window frames: warm natural wood (honey oak tone) with a grid muntin pattern (6–9 pane grid per window).
• Window glass: warm golden-amber glow transmitting through the glass, suggesting bright daylight outside. The glass should feel luminous and sun-filled, not gray or cold.

Furniture & Objects:
• Replicate all furniture from the plan in its exact position. Apply this material language:
  — Sofas and armchairs: light gray or warm off-white upholstery with exposed natural wood frames (oak or birch).
  — Dining table and chairs: natural light wood, simple Scandinavian profiles.
  — Bed: white/cream bedding with layered texture (duvet, pillows, throw), warm wood bed frame and matching nightstands with small lamps.
  — Desk/study: light wood desk surface, task chair, desk lamp, monitor or laptop, books.
  — Coffee table and side tables: light natural wood, low-profile.
  — Storage credenzas and shelving: white with wood accents, populated with books, objects, and decor.
• OBJECT DENSITY (Critical): Every surface that would logically hold objects in real life must have them. Bookshelves filled with books, kitchen counters with appliances and items, nightstands with lamps and small objects, bathroom vanity with toiletries, desk with work items. The space must feel inhabited, warm, and realistically lived-in — not sparse or staged.
• Include 4–6 potted green plants distributed naturally — large floor plants (fiddle leaf fig, monstera) near windows and corners, small potted herbs or succulents on shelves and counters.
• Area rugs under living room seating and beside the bed — neutral cream or light gray, flat-woven texture.

Lighting:
• Bright warm natural daylight entering through all windows, casting clearly defined diagonal sun patches across the wood floor.
• Windows should glow with warm golden-amber light, as if the sun is directly behind them.
• Sun angle from the upper-left of the image, creating long warm light shafts that fall diagonally across the floor toward the lower-right.
• Color temperature: 3800K–4200K (warm golden white).
• Soft ambient fill in shadowed areas — no zone is completely dark. Cool-toned ambient shadow for contrast with warm sunlight.
• Gentle contact shadows under all furniture, grounding every object to the floor.
• Overall mood: bright, airy, inviting — late morning residential with warm golden-hour quality.

Color Palette:
• Dominant: bright white walls + warm golden maple wood floors.
• Secondary: light grays (sofa upholstery, bed throw), natural light wood tones (all furniture).
• Accents: muted sage and forest greens (indoor plants), soft cream and off-white (textiles, rugs, bedding), small pops of matte black (hardware, light fixtures, chair frames, screen bezels), warm stainless steel (kitchen).
• Exterior shell: cool blue-gray slab and perimeter wall exterior.
• No saturated or bold colors anywhere. Overall tone: warm Japandi minimalism — Japanese-Scandinavian residential.

Background:
• Pure white (#FFFFFF). No gradient, no ground plane, no environment, no context.
• The floor plan floats as an isolated 3D dollhouse model on a blank white field.
• No drop shadow beneath the building slab.
• Generous white negative space on all four sides.

Quality: Photorealistic at the level of a high-end 3D architectural visualization or premium real estate marketing render. Materials respond to light with accurate reflections, soft shadows, and surface detail. The image should look like a professional photograph of a meticulously handcrafted architectural scale model. Crisp anti-aliased geometry edges, high resolution suitable for print and large-format digital presentation.
Output: PNG or TIFF.

Avoid: Preserving the input image camera angle or orientation, flat top-down extrusion of the plan, altering floor plan geometry or layout in any way, inventing rooms or furniture not shown in the plan, removing any perimeter walls entirely, perspective distortion or lens effects, dark or dramatic or moody lighting, saturated or bold accent colors, empty or sparse surfaces, cartoon or illustrative style.

label: 2D Floor Plan to Premium Executive Architectural Board
prompt:
Goal: Transform the uploaded floor plan into a premium executive architectural board in A1 horizontal format, with a contemporary minimalist aesthetic inspired by high-end international architecture studios, while maintaining absolute fidelity to the original geometry, dimensions, spatial organization, and layout.

Presentation Content:
Create a sophisticated technical presentation including:
• Complete floor plan with internal and external dimensions
• Room names and area calculations in square meters
• Structural axes and level indications
• Humanized furniture layout
• Floor patterning and technical material indications
• Area schedules
• Longitudinal and transverse schematic sections
• Small interior elevations
• Graphic scale and north arrow
• Minimalist technical title block
• Organized legends

Graphic Style:
• Precise thin linework
• Elegant graphic hierarchy
• Subtle shadows and light ambient occlusion
• Neutral tones with natural wood accents
• Ultra-clean visual composition
• Resembling real architectural documentation produced by a contemporary international architecture office

Geometry Rules:
• Maintain absolute fidelity to the original floor plan geometry, dimensions, spatial organization, and layout.
• Do not add, remove, or rearrange any rooms, walls, openings, or furniture.
• The uploaded floor plan is the single source of truth for all spatial data.

Output: High-resolution, presentation-ready image suitable for A1 print format.

Avoid: Cheap render aesthetics, visual clutter, saturated colors, student-style presentation, altering floor plan geometry or layout.

label: 3D Model to Photorealistic Competition Render
prompt:
Generate a photorealistic architectural rendering of the model while keeping all geometry, proportions, and details exactly the same as provided.

Geometry:
• Do not alter building massing, dimensions, or façade layout.

Materials & Lighting:
• Apply realistic materials, textures, and lighting to the existing geometry.
• Glass should be transparent with natural reflections.
• Concrete and metal surfaces should show accurate texture.
• All edges must remain sharp and aligned.
• Use natural daylight illumination with soft shadows for realism, maintaining a clear atmosphere.

Quality: Ultra-high resolution, sharp focus, and competition-board level photoreal quality.

label: Glass Curtain Wall + Lobby Ambience
prompt:
Enhance the ground floor bridge area by refining the glass curtain wall and doors to appear transparent with natural reflections.

Interior Lighting:
• Add soft ambient interior lighting behind the glass to reveal the lobby space.
• Show warm illumination, furniture silhouettes, and subtle depth without overpowering the façade.
• Ensure the lighting creates a welcoming glow that spills slightly onto the sidewalk, while maintaining the realism of glass reflections.

Context: Keep all other building geometry, façade materials, and surroundings exactly the same.

Quality: Ultra-high-resolution, sharp focus, competition-board photoreal quality.
Output: PNG or TIFF.

Avoid: Overexposed interior glow, visible light sources behind glass, altering building geometry or façade proportions.

label: Full Building Brick Texture Replacement
prompt:
Replace all façades of the building with a consistent red brick texture.

Brick Details:
• Ensure accurate mortar lines, brick coursing, and realistic material depth.
• Maintain all building geometry, proportions, and architectural detailing.

Integration:
• Integrate the brick texture seamlessly with existing windows, doors, and vertical fins (if still retained in their material).
• Ensure natural lighting, shadows, and reflections.

Context: Keep all surrounding streetscape elements, paving, bollards, landscaping, cars, pedestrians, and context unchanged.

Quality: Ultra-high-resolution, sharp focus, competition-board photoreal quality.
Output: PNG or TIFF.

Avoid: Inconsistent brick color across façades, losing window/door detail under the new texture, altering building geometry or proportions.

label: Material ID Generation
prompt:
Goal: Generate a Material ID image (material mask pass) from the provided base image. The output should clearly isolate and color‑code each distinct material type with solid, flat colors for use in post‑production and material editing workflows.

Instructions:
• Use the provided base image as the exact geometry and visual reference.
• Do not change the camera angle, perspective, geometry, or composition.
• Replace realistic textures, lighting, and shading with simple, flat material ID colors.
• Each unique material should be represented by a single, distinct, uniform color.

Material Separation (Examples):
Assign one flat color per material type, such as:
• Glass (windows, curtain wall)
• Metal panels / mullions
• Concrete (structure, slabs)
• Brick (if present)
• Wood (decking, cladding)
• Ground / paving
• Roof surfaces
• Vegetation / landscaping
• Water (if present)
• Railings and secondary elements
Colors should be clearly distinguishable, non‑gradient, and non‑shaded.

Rendering Rules:
• No lighting, shadows, reflections, gradients, or textures.
• No ambient occlusion.
• No transparency—glass should be a solid color.
• Edges should be clean and well‑defined.
• Adjacent materials must not share similar colors.

Style & Format:
• Flat, graphic, high‑contrast ID pass look.
• No artistic or realistic effects.
• Output should feel like a technical material mask, not a presentation image.

Output Requirements:
• High‑resolution image.
• Crisp edges and consistent fills.
• Suitable for selection masks in Photoshop or material assignment in 3D workflows.
• Preferred format: PNG or TIFF.

Avoid:
• Photorealism
• Shading or color gradients
• Blurred edges
• Overlapping or ambiguous material regions
• Changing or simplifying geometry

label: Minimal Landscape Design — Full Streetscape
prompt:
Create a minimal, thoughtfully designed landscape treatment along the sidewalks, as if designed by a professional landscape architect.

Planting:
• Add small-scale trees at regular intervals with slender trunks and controlled canopies that do not obstruct building views.
• Incorporate subtle planting beds or low groundcover integrated with paving edges for a clean, modern look.

Pedestrian Flow:
• Maintain pedestrian flow and accessibility while softening the urban edges.
• Ensure the planting is realistic, proportional, and in harmony with the architecture.

Context: Keep all other building geometry, façade details, cars, bollards, paving, and surrounding context exactly the same.

Quality: Ultra-high-resolution, sharp focus, competition-board photoreal quality.
Output: PNG or TIFF.

Avoid: Overly dense planting that obscures the building façade, unrealistic tree scale, altering building geometry.

label: Minimal Landscape — Between Glass Facade & Sidewalk Only
prompt:
Create a minimal, thoughtfully designed landscape treatment between building glass facade and sidewalks only.

• Add small-scale trees at regular intervals that do not obstruct building views, while softening the urban edges.
• Ensure the planting is realistic, proportional, and in harmony with the architecture.

Context: Keep all other building geometry, façade details, cars, bollards, paving, and surrounding context exactly the same.

Quality: Ultra-high-resolution, sharp focus, competition-board photoreal quality.

Avoid: Overly dense planting that obscures building views, unrealistic tree scale, altering building geometry.

label: Planter & Landscaping Harmonization
prompt:
Adjust the planters and landscaping at the bottom-right of the image to harmonize with the overall streetscape and building context.

• Ensure consistent scale, style, and material with the surrounding minimal landscape and small trees.
• Integrate planter materials, soil, and plants naturally with the paving, sidewalk edges, and pedestrian flow.

Context: Keep all building geometry, façades, paving patterns, bollards, cars, and surrounding context unchanged.

Quality: Ultra-high-resolution, sharp focus, competition-board photoreal quality.

Avoid: Altering planter positions or streetscape layout, unrealistic plant scale, changing building geometry.

label: Premium Paving Pattern Replacement
prompt:
Replace the plain grey street and sidewalk paving with a thoughtfully designed paving pattern by a landscape architect.

Paving Design:
• Use high-quality stone or concrete pavers arranged in a sophisticated, modern design — with variation in tone, texture, and jointing to create rhythm and hierarchy.
• Integrate subtle linear accents, bands, or geometric motifs that guide pedestrian flow and complement the building façade.
• Ensure the paving feels premium, well-detailed, and seamlessly integrated with planters, trees, and the overall streetscape.

Context: Maintain all building geometry, vegetation, and urban context unchanged.

Quality: Ultra-high-resolution, sharp focus, competition-board photoreal quality.
Output: PNG or TIFF.

Avoid: Overly uniform or repetitive paver patterns, unrealistic scale of pavers, altering building geometry or site layout.

label: Populate Interior Window Lights — Night Scene
prompt:
Goal: Render the provided image into a photorealistic night-time visualization where every residential unit window across all buildings is illuminated.

Occupancy:
• Show the buildings as fully occupied with 100% of residential windows lit.
• Keep all other elements of the scene exactly the same.

Geometry & Context:
• Use the provided image as the base.
• Maintain all building geometry, façade proportions, massing, camera angle, streetscape, and background context exactly as shown.

Window Lighting:
• Illuminate 100% of all residential windows across all buildings using warm, soft interior lighting typical of residential occupancy at night.
• Ensure brightness feels realistic — visible through glass but not overexposed.
• Apply natural variation in intensity across units so the façade doesn’t look uniform or artificial.

Curtain Treatment:
• Treat all residential units as fully occupied with curtains closed or partially closed, still allowing interior glow to be visible.
• Maintain a natural mix of curtain opacities (sheer, semi-opaque) for realism.

Environment:
• Preserve the original night-time environment: existing street lights, reflective behavior on glazing, same shadow direction, same building material colors.
• Ensure interior glow interacts naturally with glazing: soft diffusion through tinted glass, subtle reflections, correct shadowing on mullions and frames.

Quality: Ultra-high resolution.
Output: PNG or TIFF.

Avoid: Changing building geometry or camera angle, over-bright or blown-out interiors, stylized/cartoon lighting, leaving any residential window unlit.

label: Populate Scene with People — Active Street Life
prompt:
Goal: Populate the scene with realistic human activity to create a vibrant, dynamic architectural visualization.

Geometry & Context:
• Maintain the original building geometry, camera angle, and overall site context exactly as shown.

People:
• Add approximately 100 photorealistic people throughout the scene, diverse in age, ethnicity, and attire.
• Show natural activities such as walking, commuting, and interacting casually, as if people are going to work.
• Include several individuals riding bicycles along designated paths or near the plaza.
• Ensure realistic scale, lifelike textures, and varied postures for authenticity.
• Distribute people naturally across the scene without overcrowding key architectural elements.

Quality: Ultra-high-resolution, photorealistic quality suitable for competition boards.
Output: PNG or TIFF.

Avoid: Overexposed lighting, unrealistic glow effects, altering building geometry.

label: Window Residential Lighting + Unit Definition
prompt:
• Add light within the building and define each window with a residential space behind it.
• Don’t add any bollards.
• Add clarity in details.

label: Apply Style from Reference Image (Image 2 → Image 1)
prompt:
Goal: Render Image 1 in a dynamic, photorealistic architectural style by adopting the environment, materials, and design elements from Image 2.

Geometry:
• Preserve the original building geometry, façade proportions, and overall layout of Image 1 while integrating the visual style of Image 2.

Lighting & Tone:
• Apply the lighting conditions, color tone, and overall ambiance from Image 2 with natural daylight and balanced brightness.

Materials (from Image 2):
• Transparent glass with natural reflections
• Accurate brickwork with visible mortar depth and weathering
• Metal and concrete with realistic texture

Streetscape (from Image 2):
• Replicate the street landscaping from Image 2: trees, planters, and paving details for consistency.
• Maintain a clean pedestrian zone with no cars and no bollards.

Ground Floor:
• Enhance ground-floor retail areas with subtle interior glow lighting and realistic interior details for depth.

Context: Keep all other building geometry and site context from Image 1 unchanged.

Quality: Ultra-high-resolution, sharp focus, competition-board photoreal quality.

Avoid: Overexposed lighting, unrealistic glow effects, altering building proportions.

label: AI Site Analysis Diagram — Proposed Building
prompt:
Create a clean, presentation-ready architectural site analysis diagram from a Google Maps aerial image. The diagram should communicate the urban context, environmental analysis, circulation, and proposed building massing in a clear architectural graphic style suitable for feasibility studies, design presentations, or urban analysis boards. The graphic style should be minimal, vector-based, and diagrammatic, similar to presentation diagrams produced by BIG, Foster + Partners, OMA, or SOM.

[FILL IN BEFORE USE]
• Project Site Address: {SITE_ADDRESS}
• City / Region: {CITY / STATE / COUNTRY}
• Site Boundary: The project site is outlined with a bold red boundary line.
• Proposed Building: A {BUILDING_HEIGHT}-story {BUILDING_TYPE} building should be placed on the marked site.

Base Map: Use a Google Maps aerial view, map oriented north up, surrounding urban context visible, streets/blocks/landmarks visible, zoom level showing several surrounding blocks. Remove most labels. Simplify into clean vector base. Buildings: soft neutral grey. Roads: light grey lines. Open space: soft sage green. Water: pale desaturated blue.

Site Highlight: Bold red outline + soft pastel red fill + radial gradient influence zone. Label: PROJECT SITE.

Proposed Building: Simple extruded white/light grey massing block, clearly taller than context, slight shadow. Label: Proposed {BUILDING_HEIGHT}-Story {BUILDING_TYPE}.

Environmental Analysis: Sun Path arc (East to West, yellow sun icons, curved directional arrow). Sight Lines: dashed radial lines to major landmarks, skyline views, important features.

Circulation: Vehicular access — bold black arrows, highlight primary roads. Pedestrian movement — thin black arrows along pathways and sidewalks.

Urban Context Labels: Major streets, parks, landmarks, transit stations, waterfront, public spaces. Thin leader lines. North arrow + scale bar.

Avoid: Photorealistic imagery, overly detailed textures, cluttered labels.

label: Studio-Style E-Commerce Product Photo
prompt:
Transform this product photo into high quality, studio-style images.

• Use soft balanced lighting.
• Remove any background distractions.
• Apply subtle color corrections.
• Give it a clean, modern look suitable for an e-commerce catalog.
• Keep the product shape, texture and colors true to life.
